texto: She [Cressida] knows it is men's sexual desire that makes women "angels" before they have been able to possess them; once possessed, women are "things" [Troilus and Cressida I.2, 225-28, 233-34].

texto: [Talking about Rosalind in As You Like It] She disguises herself as a boy, drops all the covering inhibitions of "femininity", and really searches for her true self (...) Her disguise gives her the ability to find out about herself, what she really thinks and feels (...). And she can do all this freely, without having anyone in power tell her how women should or should not behave.(...)
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Tina Packer: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “[Talking about Rosalind in As You Like It] She disguises herself as a boy, drops all the covering…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, feminism, women pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de women e women-in-shakespeare-s-plays.

texto: He [Shakespeare] was a wordsmith who loved to act and to see things from many points of view.(...) His genius lay in being able to see all sides of an argument.

texto: The Roman Empire, born out the Roman Republic, with its ideas of democracy among a certain group of wealthy men (no vote for men without land -as with our Founding Fathers- and certainly no vote for women and slaves. Why are democracies built on top of one form of slavery or another?)
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Tina Packer que provoca temas ligados a familia. Ao questionar “The Roman Empire, born out the Roman Republic, with its ideas of democracy among a certain group …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre familia, democracy, slavery, com foco em slavery e women-s-rights.

texto: Men and women in their very essence -in their souls if you wish- have natural parity. (...) This was a relatively new idea at the time [of Shakespeare]. It ran counter to the teaching in the Bible -Eve's being made out of Adam's rib to be his helpmate -which was the basis for the idea, held for so long, that women do not have souls of their own but are dependent on their fathers' and husbands' .
